title: 2025/10/30 - PP (Part 1)
displayTitle: 2025-10-29 - Primary Production Committee - Part 1 [Subcommittee A, Part 1]
description: Thursday, 30 October 2025 | Primary Production Subcommittee A Part 1 Animal Welfare (Regulations for Management of Pigs) Amendment Bill - hearing of evidence Gillian Coumbe KC Ashley O'Driscoll Danielle Duffield Freshpork New Zealand Limited Sarah Harris Leone Brown Su-Wuen Ong
descriptionSummary: Part 1. Animal Welfare (Regulations for Management of Pigs) Amendment Bill. Gillian Coumbe KC

chunks.text: Welcome, Jillian. Good morning, Jillian. Can you, can you hear us?

Are you there? Yes, I am. I am. Good morning. Good morning. Thank you so much for joining us this morning. Uh, we're very grateful for your time. Uh, we have you for 10 minutes as far as submission process, um, and we're looking forward to your comments and opening remarks. And please, if you are kind enough to leave us a little bit of time for questions that would be gratefully received. Thank. Thank you.

Thank you. Now, my interest in this is that I was counsel for the applicants in the 2020 judicial review case, which I did on a pro bono basis. There was a lot of work involved. We were successful in obtaining a ruling that the use of farrowing crates is unlawful. Now, I oppose this bill and I have eight points to make briefly in my 10 minute challenge, as well as some redrafting recommendations, but I do hope you'll also read, uh, my full submission at some stage.

One, I'm really appalled that despite the court's clear directions to the minister and to NPI, this bill will postpone any change at all for another 10 years. And I ask, where's the respect for the court and for the rule of law? Two, the new system that will come into place in 10 years will still be noncompliant with the act.

Now, that's clearly NACS view, and although there was a suggestion from the committee yesterday that Naac may have revised its view or fallen into line, there has been no change. NACS letter to the minister of five April this year needs to be read in full, not just a sentence selectively taken out of context.

Three, why 10 years? In my submission, that's arbitrary and excessive in June this year, MPI in New Zealand pork. And I referred to these documents in my submission, were both comfortable. That, and I quote, five years was sufficient and appropriate.

It seems that the minister on a whim has extended it to 10 years just to match the phase in time of the rather measly increase in grower space. To me, this is a nonsense. I ask, does he understand the act? Does he understand his obligations under it?

Four, this urgency that we now have is self-inflicted. MPI advised the minister, and again, the documents are referred to in my submission, advise the minister two years ago of the power in the act to extend the regulations for another five years.

He did nothing. Now it's too late. And yet we see in the explanatory note to the bill and in the regulatory impact statement, a statement that this extension power has already been exercised. That's a deliberately false statement to cabinet, to parliament and to the public.

Five. I suggest that the time for wallowing, if I can use that word in continuing debate about the science has surely long gone. It's been 26 years. The high court ruled that faring crates are non-compliant end of, in our high court case, we had independent evidence from an internationally regarded expert, Dr. Emma Baxter from the University of Edinburgh.

She gave uncontested evidence that the mortality rate across all farrowing systems is roughly the same. Yes, I agree that in farrowing crates, sorry, without farrowing crates, more piglets get squashed. But with farrowing crates, more piglets die of starvation or are stillborn.

The real issue for the industry, I would suggest is one of infrastructure cost six conflicts of interest. What's extraordinary in this case is that the minister was not only effectively AEE stakeholder in 2022, he's now the decision maker because federated farmers gave a substantive submission in April, 2022 when this minister was the president of that organization.

That submission opposed all of suggestions, was highly critical of Naac and also endorsed fully New Zealand pork's proposals and recommended that the Minister now consent co consult just with New Zealand pork. Uh, there's also the minister's, you know, more generally his interests in, in the dairy industry.

I haven't seen any public disclosure of any declared conflict of interest or measures to address that, such as transfers of responsibility, blind trusts, and the like. Seven, Excuse me, Jillian, I don't mean to interrupt you, but we, we, we do have to wind up if you are capable. Thank you.

I'm sorry. I just have a few more points to make. No, no, no. Please apply. If you could finish those, that would be lovely. Thank you so much. Okay. Seven. Ordinary New Zealanders have to observe the laws farmers of other kinds of livestock have to outdoor pig farmers have. Why not this particular industry group? We are not just talking about mom and dad farmers, some pork.

A a heavyweight player is wholly Australian owned. The, the la the largest, uh, pork producer in Australasia eight. I've suggested that the issues around this government's arrangements with sun pork need further investigation. Um, they want to roll out their maternity ring. It uses the same space as a farrowing crate.

That's a coincidence. Um, of course that would rule out NACS recommendations if that is to happen. And I've raised the question whether the oi the Overseas investment office consent in 2022, uh, was a special condition of that, that be a new suncorp's new infrastructure be rolled out.

I'd like to know if that was a reference to their, um, maternity ring. My recommendations that to address the urgency, all you need to do in this bill is to have an extension of the regulations for up to five years. That would do it. The matter could then continue under the act in the normal way, preferably with responsibility given to a different minister.

Another alternative, still just an extension up to five years, uh, with schedule two of the bill, uh, amended to adopt recommendations either option in the long term in my submission, will lead to an outcome that is, that is more to adopt the minister's own phrasing, sensible, practical, and affordable.

Thank you. Mark. I Had a quick question. Jillian. Sorry. I, um, I apologize. We, we, these were five minute slots. We've met time, uh, and don't have the, the, the luxury of the time. I wish we had, uh, to ask you any questions. So I hope you have a lovely day. Uh, Mr. Chair, just, we are running a little bit ahead of time.

Can I ask one really fast question? It's quick. Yeah. Um, yes, please. Quick. Yeah. It's just given the nature of the submission. Um, thanks, Jillian. My question is very quick. At the top of your submission, you said that, uh, you think the minister, the, the law would actually still be unlawful. Is that your legal, um, view that, uh, this would actually still open up legal risk for the industry?

Yes. If, if the proposal that's to come into force, the new regulations that are supposed to come into force in 10 days were made by regulation in the ordinary way, instead of being enshrined in an act of parliament, I would have another successful judicial review. Thank you. Great answer. Thank you so much, Jillian.
Have thanks again. Have a lovely day. Thank You. Thank you so much. We now go across to Ashley o Driscoll for five minutes.

Hello. Good morning. Good morning. Thank you so much for joining us this morning. Uh, we have you, Ashley, for five minutes. I'm pretty sure you know how this works. Um, you tell us what you think and if you can allow us a bit of time for questions in your five minute slot. That's entirely up to you. Thank you so much.

Um, I'd like to thank the committee for taking the time to consider my submission. I've prepared a statement that's about three minutes long, so a little bit of time at the end. Mm-hmm. Um, so my name is Ashley o Driscoll, and I'm a veterinarian and a beef farmer. I've worked along MPI as an expert witness on a large number of animal welfare cases over the past four years.

In 2022, I was involved in an animal welfare investigation with MPI on a conventional chicken egg farm. Conventional layer cages are known as battery cages, which have been phased out New Zealand due to the fact that these cages severely restrict a chicken's avail ability to perform their most normal behaviors. At the time of the investigation, battery cages had not yet been phased out, but the welfare concern in this case was overcrowding.

The overcrowding situation of these chickens was successfully prosecuted by MPI on the basis that the persons in charge failed to allow hens the opportunity to display normal patterns of behavior, which directly relates to one of the five foundational requirements in section four of the New Zealand Animal Welfare Act.

In the sentencing summary, normal behavior was further defined as standing, turning around stretching wings, brooding and walking. Barrowing and mating crates prohibit the same normal behaviors of turning around and walking among other limited behaviors. The usage of these crates may increase profitability and decrease piglet death, but this is not an acceptable justification for the distress experience by thousands of sows.

The standard usage of battery cages also increased profitability and decreased he death, but the emphasis was correctly placed on removing the distress caused by the inhibition of normal movement. The extended use of crates is not in line with our values. As a country as recognized during the High CR Court of New Zealand grueling, the layer industry managed to phase out battery cages over a period of five years at a significant cost to the industry.

I fail to understand why the pork industry requires a further 10 years to complete the transition away from extended usage of farrowing and mating crates. During that period, thousands of individual pigs will continue experience to experience weeks of unreasonable distress and frustration due to their inability to perform some of their most basic behaviors.

I spend a large portion of my time ensuring that minimum welfare standards are met for various species of animals within New Zealand. The stated objective of this bill is to quote, ensure that locally produced pork products meet good animal welfare practices. It is my strong professional opinion that a failure to provide the structure and requirements for a quick transition away from extended usage of crates is a failure to provide minimum animal welfare, let alone good animal welfare. Thank you.

Thank you. Very good submission and, and, and quite succinct, um, um, actually, very grateful for it. Very quick question. As a veterinarian, um, would you appreciate and be willing to accept on farm visitation? We, free range pigs have disproportionately killed their piglets in farrowing.

Um, I, I I I, I'm struck by your rationale when we're trying to remove, uh, uh, said risk to animal welfare. And I think you're right about the sos I think this is something that we've really gotta grapple with. But how do you square as a professional going on farm in a free range environment and saying, I now see a lot of dead piglets.

Do you think in your future, that's something that you could come to accept if that was the case? So I think what you're asking is, like I said, the same thing that was asked about battery cages. So the prevalence of disease and mortality is higher in, in situations that are not battery cages.

However, that is also, that's at the expense of lifelong suffering as far as freedom of movement goes. Mm-hmm. So, as your previous speaker said, there is, it's not, it's not a huge, so I don't work within the pork industry, so I can't quote you numbers, but it's not a huge increase in piglet mortality, but I recognize that it's there.

Yes. It's just not, it's, it's not justifiable for the weeks that, that these animals are, are not allowed to move. The severity of the welfare impacts are not justifiable. So For pig loss, you'll, you'll have pig lot loss. So, very quickly, Ashley, you, you're saying you acknowledge that there will be an increase of piglet mortality, correct?

Oh, yeah. I acknowledge that in my statement. Yep. That's well known as it was in the layer industry. Yep, Yep, yep. Okay. Very good. Thank you so much. Very, very grateful. Um, it's always lovely to have you people, I wish we could talk to you more, but we've got a a whole line of of, of people we've got to get through today that are of different opinions.

So thank you, Ashley. Very grateful for your time and very succinct delivery. Yep. Um, and, and go. Well, thank you so much. Thank you. Thank you. Uh, we now move across to Danielle Darfield. Sure. Hello. Hello. Welcome. Danielle, this, uh, lovely to have you here.

I believe we have you for five minutes. So I will leave it with you. You can tell us your thoughts and ideas, and if you would like questions, please leave a bit of time at the end. Thank you, mark. Yeah. So thank you for listening to me today. I'm an adjunct lecturer of animal law at the University of Otago, and I've been an animal law practitioner for a number of years, including, uh, as the former president of the New Zealand Animal Law Association.

And like many others who have spoken on this bill, I'm very concerned by it. I oppose it. And I have five points I'd like to make. So, firstly, this bill completely undermines the decision of the high court and the purpose of the Animal Offer Act. The country's leading piece of animal offer legislation. So as we heard just before from Jillian co kc, there was a 2020 decision by the high court in which Fahr crates were found to be illegal.

And this result in a phase out period, which this bill now sidesteps, and I completely echo the comments made by Jillian Co, uh, in relation to the concerns around the rule of law, um, in terms of a bill completely undermining their judgment. So secondly, the real problem with this piece of legislation is it deprives the 2015 amendment to the Animal Welfare Act of its main effect and purpose.

So one of the main purposes of the Animal Welfare Act is to allow animals to express normal patterns of behavior. And prior to 2015, there was a provision in the act which allowed in exceptional circumstances, practices to continue, which did not allow animals to express normal patterns of behavior. And this was routinely used to justify certain practices like pH crates and battery cages, um, which actually, um, do not meet the obligations of the act.

And that's why Parliament amended the ACT in 2015, right, and inserted this new regime under Section 1 8 3 A to require non-compliant practices to be clearly phased out with strict time limits. And this bill completely ignores those time limits and are a few in this regard to a couple of key comments from Justice Lin, her high court judgment, in which she said it was plain that Parliament's intention in passing this amendment was to ensure that noncompliant practices were to be time limited up to fi 10 years only, and ultimately phased out,

and that any extension of time was to be limited to up to five years once only. I also refer you to her comment at paragraph 84, that Frates were explicitly given as an example of a non-compliant practice in the explanatory note to the 2015 bill, um, which Section 180 3 sought to address.

So this bill is effectively saying Section 180 3 applies except when we don't want it to, and that's very problematic. Thirdly, this bill continues beyond 2035 practices, which I think by any reasonable interpretation, are unnecessary within the meaning of the Animal Welfare Act. We all know that half of pig farmers already farm without the use of f crates in New Zealand, and they abandoned a number of countries like Sweden, Switzerland, and Norway, which you know, are clearly leading, um, ahead of New Zealand, and which obviously raises some concerns

for New Zealand's international reputation with respect to animal welfare as well. And I echo completely Jillian COO's concerns that Naac was a government's expert committee on animal welfare, has this view, and yet it's been ignored. Fourthly, this bill has been subject to significant procedural defects.

It has not been subject to a proper consultation. Now, NAAC consulted on two options in their draft pick code in 2022, free faring or temporary creatine for up to 72 hours. And public consultations are a key principle under the Animal Offer Act. They're required. And that is because there is enormous public interest in the treatment of animals in art.

And yet these proposals as put forward in this bill, have not been a proper properly consulted. And this, uh, rush through piece of legislation is not an adequate substitute for that. Firstly, I'm pretty concerned, uh, by what I've seen in the news subsequent to filing my written submission, which is come out through Official Information Act request, which gives pretty strong indications of regulatory capture, how this is a piece of public interest legislation.

The Animal Welfare Act, its purpose is to uphold the public's belief in the humane treatment of animals. And Justice Kell in her high court decision emphasize that practicality and economic impact are not the primary purpose of the legislation and they cannot override the welfare obligations of the act. But this bill seems to ignore that. Um, and I am rather concerned by the influence that New Zealand pork seems to have had, uh, on this legislation.

It seems unduly large, it seems more significant in nacs own impact despite them being the, you know, key committee under the legislation for recommending animal welfare changes. So those are the main, my main points and I'm, I'm happy to take any questions. Members, we have about a minute left to, um, have questions if Dan Daniel would like to field any from, uh, my team.
Please speak up now because we've got Danielle for about a minute.

Uh, Danielle, there appear to be no questions. Thank you so much for your submission. We are very grateful for your submission. Um, it's very succinct and your delivery. Um, so I hope you have a great day and thank you for your thoughts. Thank you for your thoughts and ideas. Thank you very much. Take care. Have a great day. Team.

Uh, look, fresh Pork is a family owned business, um, that has been operating for over 40 years.

We currently employ about 150 people. Um, we partner with about 20 farmers in the South island. Uh, most of those farmers are also family owned businesses. Combined with our various partners, we estimate our contribution to the New Zealand economy to be more than 350 million. We're currently domestic only.

Uh, and, uh, we're proud to feed Kiwis right around, uh, the country. With high quality New Zealand growing pork, fresh pork. New Zealand fully supports the continuous improvement of our industry, and we support regulations and standards that best enabled this to happen both now and into the future. We are in support of the proposed animal welfare amendment bill on the basis that we believe that provides the best science-based welfare position, uh, for both the sour and the piglet, along with, uh, an appropriate timeframe

to make what will be significant changes for the industry. Fresh port also believes that in conjunction with proposed changes, that it is critical that all pork consumed here in New Zealand must meet our New Zealand wealthiest standards. Today, that is not the case with about 60% of pork consumed in New Zealand coming from other countries, most of which does not meet our current standards, nevermind the new standards that are being proposed that New Zealand wants to make the biggest improvement in pig welfare.

And short core imports also meet our welfare standards. There's a real risk that without requiring imports to meet our new standards, that we will see more New Zealand farmers exit our industry is they simply can't compete. In doing so, we will effectively offshore the welfare standards by allowing low lower welfare imports to fill the demand gap created.

We are not asking to ban imported pork. We are saying, what we are saying is that we need an equal playing field and strongly request that this is put in place at the same time as the new New Zealand Pig welfare code. We also support the need to improve our country of our origin labeling on products.

However, this is required to help the New Zealand consumer be better informed about where their products come from. It should not be a substitute to allow lower welfare pork to be imported into New Zealand. The last five years have been, uh, particularly frustrating for the New Zealand pork industry.

Uh, I can generally say that, uh, this latest welfare code review has created significant uncertainty for New Zealand pig farmers and, uh, everyone else in the value chain, including ourselves. We've effectively been in limbo for the last five years, waiting for the new code to be confirmed. Farmers haven't known where the welfare code was going to land, so it's been impossible for them to make an informed call on how or what to invest in on farm.

While we've heard, uh, various other submissions, uh, during this process refer to farmers having five years from 2020 to comply with the changes, we, we believe this simply is not the case. How can we expect farmers to invest hundreds of thousands of dollars, if not millions of dollars without knowing what the new peak welfare code is and what they need to be doing?

I'd suggest not many would do this and banks would struggle to support funding requirements. This limbo period has stifled investment in our industry at time when many want to invest in improvements and modernization of their operations. This has been the same for Fresh Port New Zealand, who is wanting to progress our plans to modernize our facilities and also become export capable.

We require a well-balanced pig welfare code to provide us with long-term confidence to invest and continue to progress our domestic industry. While I won't go into the specific details, the co conclusions reached and, and I will quote from the executive summary, um, regarding farrowing systems. The literature suggests that temporary creating systems in which farrowing crate use is limited to two to three days pre farrowing until four to five days post farrowing.

And providing sows with deformable and manipulative materials with which to build a nest allows the sour to display normal patterns of behavior, thereby limiting sour stress while reducing peri and postnatal piglet mortality, including crushing and salvaging regarding space allowance for grower pigs.

The statistical tools available to analyze available literature provide no evidence that productivity or welfare manage, uh, measurements will be improved with a K value greater than 0.03. Accordingly, we conclude that the need to display normal patterns of behavior does not on the evidence presently available, require increasing the minimum K value.
